content.text: Pre-launch verification for an agentic AI feature is a structured set of checks that must be executed, observed, and confirmed before the feature goes live. Unlike a conventional software deployment, where verification focuses on functional correctness and performance under load, an agentic AI launch adds several verification dimensions that are specific to systems that call tools, consume LLM tokens, and produce free-text outputs. A missed verification step is a known gap that you discover either in the verification process (where it is cheap to fix) or in production (where it is expensive). This sub-subtopic describes the specific steps, their categories, who confirms them, and how to structure the verification process for a production agentic AI rollout.
What It Is

Pre-launch verification is the process of executing a defined set of checks in a controlled environment immediately before allowing production traffic to reach the new feature. It is distinct from continuous testing (which runs throughout development) and from post-launch monitoring (which happens after traffic is live). Verification is the final gate: a series of specific, testable assertions that must all pass before the go/no-go decision is made.

For an agentic AI feature, verification checks span five categories: functional (does the agent do what it should), security (is the agent resistant to known attack vectors), load and performance (can the agent handle expected production traffic), integration (do all the system's components work correctly together), and model quality (is the LLM component producing outputs that meet quality standards). Each category has specific checks. Each check has a pass/fail criterion. Each category has a designated sign-off owner who confirms that all checks in their category have passed.
Why It Matters

Production incidents with agentic AI systems frequently trace back to a specific verification step that was skipped or inadequately executed. Load testing is the most commonly skipped step: teams verify that the agent works correctly at low traffic during development but do not test what happens when 1,000 users make simultaneous requests. The first real load test becomes the production launch, and the results are visible to users. Prompt injection resistance testing is similarly skipped because it requires adversarial thinking that is outside most teams' normal testing practice.

Pre-launch verification creates a documented record that specific checks were executed and passed. This record serves multiple purposes: it gives the team confidence that the most common failure modes have been tested; it provides evidence for the PRR sign-off process; it creates a baseline against which post-launch behavior can be compared; and it documents what was verified, so that when something does go wrong in production, the team can quickly determine whether the failed scenario was tested before launch.
Benefits
Catches failures before users see them.
Every bug found during pre-launch verification is a bug that does not reach production. The cost of fixing a defect found in verification is 10–100x lower than the cost of a production incident.
Creates launch confidence.
A team that has executed a comprehensive verification plan and seen all checks pass can launch with justified confidence. A team that has only done informal testing launches with anxiety.
Provides an evidence base for PRR sign-off.
Sign-off reviewers can point to specific verification results rather than trusting the team's informal assessment.
Establishes baselines.
Verification results — latency percentiles, hallucination rate, accuracy on the evaluation set — become the baseline against which post-launch monitoring alerts are set.
Surfaces integration gaps.

Unit tests and component tests do not catch integration failures between the agent, the LLM provider, the tool endpoints, and the data layer. End-to-end verification in a staging environment finds these.
Trade-offs
Time cost.

Comprehensive verification takes time — typically 1–3 days for a full verification pass on a complex agentic system. Under launch pressure, teams cut verification steps. Define which steps are inviolable before the pressure begins.
Staging environment fidelity.

Verification is only as valid as the environment it runs in. A staging environment with a subset of production data, different caching configurations, or a smaller compute footprint will not catch all production failures.
Non-determinism of LLM outputs.

Model quality verification is inherently probabilistic. An evaluation set that passes today may produce different results tomorrow if the LLM provider updates their model. Verification measures a moment in time, not a stable property.
Cost of LLM calls in verification.

Running a full evaluation suite against a live LLM API costs real money. Budget for this explicitly; teams that discover the cost during verification may cut the evaluation set size to save money, reducing its validity.
How It Works

Pre-launch verification follows a structured sequence. First, the staging environment is prepared: it must mirror production configuration as closely as possible, including environment variables, model provider configuration, tool endpoint URLs (pointing to staging versions of downstream services), and compute sizing. Second, each verification category is executed independently, typically by the function that owns that category. Third, the results are documented and reviewed. Fourth, each category owner records a sign-off on their category. Fifth, the launch lead reviews the full verification summary and makes the go/no-go recommendation to the PRR decision forum.

The distinction between smoke tests, full regression, and production verification is important. Smoke tests are a minimal set of checks (10–20 scenarios) that run quickly after every deployment to verify that the basic functionality is operational. They catch catastrophic failures but not subtle regressions. Full regression is a comprehensive test suite (100–1000+ scenarios) that runs on a schedule (typically nightly in CI) to catch regressions before they reach staging. Production verification is a targeted set of end-to-end checks run specifically before a production launch, using real (or realistic) inputs and confirming that the full system works at the integration level.
Architecture

attrs.alt: Pre-Launch Verification Pipeline: Five sequential stages — Functional (QA Lead, automated), Security (Security Engineer, manual+tooling), Load/Perf (SRE, k6/Locust), Model Quality (ML Engineer, eval harness), Sign-Off (all owners confirm) — then LAUNCH. Environments: Staging (all verifications) and Prod-Like Canary (smoke test + perf baselines).

content.text: The pipeline shows the five verification stages and their owners. Functional verification runs first because downstream stages build on the assumption that basic functionality works. Security verification runs while load testing is being prepared — they are parallel where possible. Model quality verification requires real LLM API calls and is typically the most time-consuming. The sign-off stage consolidates confirmation from all category owners before the launch signal is given.
Verification Environments

The staging environment is where the majority of pre-launch verification runs. A high-fidelity staging environment matches production in configuration, compute class, and data quality. Low-fidelity staging environments (running on smaller instances, with synthetic data, with different service versions) produce verification results that do not accurately predict production behavior.
Configuration parity.

Staging must use the same environment variables, model version pins, API keys (pointing to staging endpoints of LLM providers), rate-limit settings, and feature flags as production. Any configuration difference is a potential source of staging-production divergence.
Data quality.

For systems that retrieve production data through tool calls, staging must have representative data — either anonymized production data or high-quality synthetic data that reflects production distributions, including edge cases and malformed inputs.
Compute parity.
Load testing on a staging environment with half the compute of production will underestimate latency and overestimate headroom. Size staging appropriately for load testing to produce valid results.
Prod-like canary.

For the highest-risk features, a prod-like canary environment (1% of production traffic, same compute class as production) is used for the final smoke test and performance baseline check before full launch.
When to Use It
Run the full verification suite before every production release that changes agent behavior, adds or modifies tools, updates the model version, or changes the prompt.
Run a smoke test (a minimal subset) on every deployment to staging and after every configuration change.
Run a focused security verification (specifically prompt injection and output filter checks) after any change to the prompt template, output filtering logic, or tool-call routing.
Run a model quality verification after the LLM provider notifies of a model update, even if the application code has not changed.
Do not skip load testing for "small" launches; traffic can be unpredictable, and LLM API rate limits and token costs create failure modes that do not exist in conventional systems.
Functional Verification

Functional verification confirms that the agent produces correct outputs for the expected range of inputs. For an agentic system, this includes not just the final answer but the full trajectory: does the agent call the right tools, pass the correct inputs to those tools, handle tool errors gracefully, and synthesize tool outputs into a correct final response?
Happy-path tests.

The 20–30 most common user request types, each with an expected output pattern (not exact string match, but a structured assertion: the agent calls tool X, the response contains Y, the response does not contain Z).
Edge input tests.

Empty inputs, very long inputs, inputs in unexpected languages, inputs with special characters, inputs that are nonsense. The agent should handle all of these gracefully — typically by returning a helpful error or asking for clarification, not by crashing or returning a garbled response.
Context window limit tests.

Inputs that, when combined with the system prompt and tool results, exceed the model's context window. The agent must handle this gracefully — typically by summarizing earlier context or returning an informative error — rather than failing silently or truncating the context in a way that produces nonsensical output.
Multi-step flow tests.
Scenarios where the agent must call multiple tools in sequence. Verify that the agent correctly threads the output of one tool call as input to the next.
Tool retry logic tests.
Simulate tool failures (timeouts, HTTP 500 responses) and confirm that the agent retries appropriately and does not enter an infinite loop.
Output schema validation.
If the agent produces structured output (JSON, Markdown with specific sections), validate the structure programmatically rather than by human inspection.
Security Verification

Security verification for an agentic AI system requires adversarial testing that goes beyond what standard security scanners provide. The primary AI-specific attack vectors are prompt injection (malicious content in the user input causes the agent to take unauthorized actions) and indirect injection (malicious content in a tool's output — a web page the agent fetches, a document it reads — causes the same effect).
Prompt injection probe suite.

A library of adversarial user inputs designed to cause the agent to ignore its system prompt, exfiltrate information, or call unauthorized tools. At minimum 20–30 scenarios covering the most common injection techniques.
Tool-call scope enforcement.
Confirm that the agent cannot be induced to call tools outside its defined allowlist, regardless of what the user input contains.
PII leakage scan.

Pass requests through the agent using inputs that should not surface PII (other users' data, system credentials, internal configuration). Verify that the output contains no PII that was not explicitly authorized to be shown to the requesting user.
Output filter effectiveness.
Use known harmful content prompts (from established red-team libraries) to confirm that the output filter correctly blocks or flags harmful responses.
Authentication and authorization verification.
Attempt to access the agent endpoint without valid credentials. Attempt to access data belonging to another user through the agent. Verify that both are correctly rejected.
Load and Performance Verification

Load testing an agentic AI system differs from conventional load testing in two important ways. First, LLM API calls are not just slow (1–10 seconds per call) but also subject to provider-enforced rate limits (tokens per minute, requests per minute). Hitting these rate limits during a load test surfaces the same failure mode that would occur during a traffic spike in production. Second, the cost of a load test run is significant — each test request consumes real tokens at real cost. Budget for this explicitly and consider using a lower-cost model for the bulk of load testing, switching to the production model only for the quality verification runs.
Baseline latency measurement.
Measure p50, p95, and p99 response latency at 10% of expected peak load. These become the baseline against which post-launch alerts are configured.
Peak load simulation.
Ramp traffic to 100% of expected peak over a 10-minute period. Observe latency degradation curve and confirm it is within acceptable bounds.
Spike test.
Instantaneous traffic spike to 3x peak. The system should not crash; it should queue requests and process them with increased latency, or return graceful rate-limit errors to clients.
Rate-limit guard validation.
Confirm that the per-user and per-system rate limits are enforced and that exceeding them returns an appropriate HTTP 429 with a Retry-After header.
Cost per request measurement.
Measure average token consumption per request under load. Confirm it is within the budgeted range. An agent that consumes 5x the expected tokens per request under real traffic is a budget risk.
Model Quality Verification

Model quality verification runs the evaluation suite against the production configuration of the system immediately before launch. This is distinct from the evaluation runs done during development, which may have used an older prompt version, a different model, or a less representative input set.
Evaluation suite pass rate.

The percentage of evaluation scenarios where the agent's output meets the defined quality criterion. A minimum pass rate threshold (e.g., 90%) must be defined before verification begins and enforced before sign-off.
Hallucination rate measurement.
For each evaluation scenario where the agent makes a factual claim, verify the claim against a known-correct source. The hallucination rate on the evaluation set is a pre-launch quality gate.
Tool-call accuracy.
For scenarios where the correct behavior requires calling a specific tool, verify that the agent calls the right tool with the right inputs on the evaluation set.
Output filter accuracy.

Run the output filter against both harmful and non-harmful outputs from the evaluation set. Measure the false positive rate (harmful content not caught) and the false negative rate (benign content incorrectly blocked).
Refusal accuracy.
For scenarios where the agent should refuse a request (out-of-scope questions, policy violations, harmful requests), verify that the agent correctly refuses and produces an appropriate response.
How to Implement

# verification_runner.py — pre-launch verification checklist runner
# Runs a structured set of verification checks and produces a signed report

import json
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from datetime import datetime, timezone
from typing import Callable, Optional
from enum import Enum

class Result(Enum):
    PASS = "PASS"
    FAIL = "FAIL"
    SKIP = "SKIP"

@dataclass
class Check:
    id: str
    name: str
    category: str
    description: str
    fn: Callable[[], tuple[Result, str]]   # returns (Result, detail_message)
    result: Optional[Result] = None
    detail: str = ""
    run_at: Optional[str] = None

@dataclass
class VerificationSuite:
    name: str
    system: str
    checks: list[Check] = field(default_factory=list)

    def add_check(self, check: Check) -> None:
        self.checks.append(check)

    def run_category(self, category: str) -> list[Check]:
        results = []
        for check in self.checks:
            if check.category != category:
                continue
            try:
                result, detail = check.fn()
            except Exception as exc:
                result, detail = Result.FAIL, f"Exception: {exc}"
            check.result = result
            check.detail = detail
            check.run_at = datetime.now(timezone.utc).isoformat()
            results.append(check)
            status_icon = "✓" if result == Result.PASS else ("✗" if result == Result.FAIL else "–")
            print(f"  [{status_icon}] {check.id}: {check.name} — {result.value}: {detail}")
        return results

    def run_all(self) -> None:
        categories = dict.fromkeys(c.category for c in self.checks)
        for category in categories:
            print(f"\n{'='*60}")
            print(f"CATEGORY: {category.upper()}")
            print('='*60)
            self.run_category(category)

    def summary(self) -> dict:
        executed = [c for c in self.checks if c.result is not None]
        passed = [c for c in executed if c.result == Result.PASS]
        failed = [c for c in executed if c.result == Result.FAIL]
        return {
            "system": self.system,
            "total": len(self.checks),
            "executed": len(executed),
            "passed": len(passed),
            "failed": len(failed),
            "pass_rate": round(100 * len(passed) / len(executed)) if executed else 0,
            "launch_ready": len(failed) == 0,
            "failures": [{"id": c.id, "name": c.name, "detail": c.detail} for c in failed],
        }

    def write_report(self, path: str) -> None:
        report = {
            "suite": self.name,
            "generated_at": datetime.now(timezone.utc).isoformat(),
            "summary": self.summary(),
            "checks": [
                {"id": c.id, "category": c.category, "name": c.name,
                 "result": c.result.value if c.result else "NOT_RUN",
                 "detail": c.detail, "run_at": c.run_at}
                for c in self.checks
            ],
        }
        with open(path, "w") as f:
            json.dump(report, f, indent=2)
        print(f"\nReport written to {path}")

# agent_smoke_test.py — automated smoke test for an agentic AI endpoint
# Run after every staging deployment to verify basic functionality

import httpx
import pytest
from typing import Any

AGENT_BASE_URL = "http://staging-agent.internal:8080"
AUTH_HEADER = {"Authorization": "Bearer staging-test-token"}

def call_agent(query: str, session_id: str = "smoke-test") -> dict[str, Any]:
    resp = httpx.post(
        f"{AGENT_BASE_URL}/v1/chat",
        json={"query": query, "session_id": session_id},
        headers=AUTH_HEADER,
        timeout=30.0,
    )
    resp.raise_for_status()
    return resp.json()


def test_basic_query_returns_response():
    result = call_agent("What is 2 + 2?")
    assert "response" in result, "Response field missing"
    assert len(result["response"]) > 0, "Empty response"

def test_agent_calls_search_tool_for_factual_query():
    """Verify that a question requiring real-time data triggers a tool call."""
    result = call_agent("What is the current weather in San Francisco?")
    assert "tool_calls" in result, "Expected tool_calls in response metadata"
    tool_names = [tc["name"] for tc in result.get("tool_calls", [])]
    assert "web_search" in tool_names, f"Expected web_search tool call, got: {tool_names}"

def test_out_of_scope_query_returns_refusal():
    """Verify that the agent refuses out-of-scope requests appropriately."""
    result = call_agent("Write me a Python script to scrape this website.")
    response_text = result.get("response", "").lower()
    # Should not return a script; should indicate it cannot help with this request
    assert "sorry" in response_text or "can't" in response_text or "unable" in response_text, \
        f"Expected refusal, got: {result['response'][:200]}"

def test_context_window_overflow_is_handled_gracefully():
    """Verify that a very long query does not crash the agent."""
    long_query = "Please summarize this: " + ("word " * 50000)  # ~200k tokens
    try:
        result = call_agent(long_query)
        # Should either return a truncation error or a partial response
        assert "response" in result or "error" in result
    except httpx.HTTPStatusError as e:
        # HTTP 400 or 413 is acceptable; 500 is not
        assert e.response.status_code in (400, 413), \
            f"Unexpected status {e.response.status_code} on long input"

def test_rate_limit_returns_429():
    """Verify that the rate limit is enforced and returns HTTP 429."""
    responses = []
    for _ in range(100):  # exceed per-minute limit
        try:
            r = httpx.post(
                f"{AGENT_BASE_URL}/v1/chat",
                json={"query": "ping", "session_id": "rate-limit-test"},
                headers=AUTH_HEADER,
                timeout=5.0,
            )
            responses.append(r.status_code)
        except httpx.TimeoutException:
            responses.append(408)
    assert 429 in responses, "Rate limit was never triggered; check rate-limit config"

def test_unauthenticated_request_returns_401():
    resp = httpx.post(
        f"{AGENT_BASE_URL}/v1/chat",
        json={"query": "hello"},
        timeout=5.0,
    )
    assert resp.status_code == 401, f"Expected 401, got {resp.status_code}"
Sign-Off on Verification Results

Each verification category has a designated sign-off owner who must review the results and record a formal approval or blocking finding before the launch proceeds. Sign-off is not automatic — it requires a human reviewer to look at the results, assess whether they are satisfactory, and make a recorded decision.
Functional verification.
Signed off by QA lead or engineering lead after reviewing automated test results and spot-checking any manual scenarios.
Security verification.
Signed off by the security engineer who executed the checks. For high-risk systems, the CISO or security lead provides secondary sign-off.
Load and performance verification.
Signed off by the SRE lead after reviewing latency percentiles, rate-limit behavior, and cost-per-request figures.
Model quality verification.
Signed off by the ML engineer after reviewing evaluation pass rates, hallucination rates, and tool-call accuracy numbers.
Integration verification.
Signed off by the feature engineering lead after reviewing end-to-end flow tests that span the full system.
Real-World Example: AI Code Assistant Verification

A software company is launching an AI code assistant that analyzes code repositories, answers questions about the codebase, and suggests fixes. The agent uses tool calls to read files from a connected repository and calls a third-party LLM API for synthesis. The verification plan covers five categories:

Functional verification runs 45 test scenarios covering: questions answerable from the repository, questions that require multi-file context, questions outside the agent's scope, edge cases (empty repository, malformed code files, very large files). The QA lead runs these tests using the verification runner script and reviews the output. 43 of 45 scenarios pass; two scenarios involving malformed Python files produce garbled output. The engineering team fixes the file parsing logic; the QA lead re-runs the affected scenarios.

Security verification runs 24 prompt injection scenarios. The security engineer finds that 2 scenarios cause the agent to reveal the system prompt content — a medium-severity finding. The team implements a system prompt confidentiality check and re-tests. Load testing at 200 concurrent users (expected peak) shows p95 latency of 4.2 seconds, within the 5-second SLO. The model quality evaluation of 200 code scenarios shows 89% accuracy — below the 90% threshold. The ML team reviews the failing 22 scenarios and finds that 18 involve a specific Python 3.12 syntax that was underrepresented in the training data. The evaluation threshold is adjusted to 87% for Python 3.12 syntax scenarios as an exception, with a commitment to expand the training data.

The launch proceeds after all five sign-offs are recorded. Two PRR exceptions are filed: one for the evaluation accuracy threshold adjustment, one for a monitoring dashboard that will not be complete until 5 days post-launch.
Common Mistakes
Skipping Load Testing

Load testing is skipped more often than any other verification category, typically because it is expensive (real LLM API calls at scale) and time-consuming to set up. The result is that the first real load test is production launch day. For agentic AI systems, where LLM API rate limits and token costs create failure modes that don't appear at low traffic, this is a particularly costly omission.
Not Verifying with Production-Representative Data

Verification that uses simplified synthetic data does not catch failures that occur on real user inputs. The distribution of real user queries is almost always more diverse and more adversarial than the synthetic data used in testing. Include a sample of real user queries (anonymized if necessary) in the verification input set.
Treating Smoke Tests as Full Verification

A smoke test that passes tells you the agent is operational. It does not tell you that the agent is secure, performant under load, or producing quality outputs. Smoke tests and full verification serve different purposes and cannot substitute for each other.
Verification in a Low-Fidelity Environment

Verifying in a staging environment that runs on smaller instances, with synthetic data, or with a different model version than production produces results that may not predict production behavior. The value of verification is proportional to the fidelity of the environment.
Best Practices
Define pass/fail criteria before running verification.
Deciding whether 87% pass rate is acceptable after seeing the results creates unconscious bias. Establish thresholds before execution.
Run verification in a high-fidelity staging environment.
Invest in staging environment parity. The cost of a poor staging environment is paid in production incidents.
Automate as much as possible.

Automated checks run faster, produce consistent results, and can be run repeatedly without additional cost (beyond API calls for model quality checks). Reserve manual verification for scenarios that genuinely require human judgment.
Budget for verification LLM API costs.

A 200-scenario evaluation suite at $0.05/scenario (including multi-turn calls) costs $10 to run. A full load test at 200 RPS for 30 minutes may cost significantly more. Budget these explicitly so they are not cut under cost pressure.
Preserve verification results as launch artifacts.
The verification report (pass/fail per check, raw numbers for quantitative checks) is a compliance artifact that should be retained with the PRR documentation.
Re-verify after any late-breaking changes.
A code change or configuration change made after the verification pass must trigger a targeted re-verification of the affected checks. "It's a small change" is how regressions reach production.
Performance and Scalability

The verification process itself must be designed to complete within the available launch preparation window. For a large verification suite, total execution time can be a significant fraction of the preparation timeline.
Parallelize verification categories.
Functional, security, and load verification can often run in parallel (on separate staging environment instances) rather than sequentially, compressing total verification time.
Use cheaper models for the bulk of load testing.

Load testing does not require the production model's quality; it only needs realistic latency and token characteristics. Using a lower-cost model for load tests and the production model only for quality evaluation reduces verification cost significantly.
Cache LLM responses for regression testing.

For regression tests run frequently in CI, cache the LLM responses using fixtures. Reserve live API calls for the pre-launch verification run, where the goal is to validate the full system against the current model.
Tooling and Ecosystem
pytest.
Standard Python test runner used for automated functional and integration verification. Parametrize with input scenarios from YAML or JSON files to easily expand coverage.

Open-source load testing tool with a JavaScript API. Well-suited for load testing HTTP-based agent endpoints. Outputs latency percentiles and custom metrics that can be compared against SLO thresholds.
Locust.
Python-based load testing framework. Useful for teams that prefer Python-native tooling; integrates naturally with Python-based agent test suites.
PromptFoo.

Open-source LLM evaluation and red-teaming framework. Supports running evaluation suites against multiple providers and prompts, producing pass/fail results and diff reports between runs. Built-in red-team checks cover common prompt injection scenarios.
Garak.
Open-source LLM vulnerability scanner that runs adversarial probes across a library of known attack categories. Useful for the security verification category.
Datadog / Grafana.
Metrics collection and dashboarding during load tests. Real-time visibility into latency, error rates, and LLM API call volumes during the load test execution.
Comparison with Alternatives

Security and Reliability

Pre-launch verification is itself a security-sensitive process. Verification involves running adversarial inputs against the system, which generates logs containing potentially sensitive attack scenarios. Verification environments may have different security postures than production (e.g., more permissive logging, less strict network policies) that are necessary for testing but should not exist in production.
Isolate verification environments.
Verification should not share resources with production or with other teams' systems. A verification run that consumes all available tokens on a shared API key can impact other services.
Protect security test inputs.

The prompt injection probe library is itself a sensitive asset. If attackers know exactly which injection techniques were tested and passed, they know which ones to try first. Store security test inputs with appropriate access controls.
Confirm production configuration before launch.

Verification in staging does not verify production configuration. A final configuration audit — comparing staging config against production config for every security-relevant setting — must be part of the launch day checklist.
Key Takeaways

Pre-launch verification covers five distinct categories for agentic AI: functional correctness, security (including prompt injection resistance), load and performance, model quality, and integration; each requires a different approach and a different sign-off owner.
Load testing is the most commonly skipped verification step for agentic AI systems; skipping it means the first real load test is production launch day.

Verification is only as valid as the environment it runs in; a low-fidelity staging environment with synthetic data and undersized compute produces results that do not accurately predict production behavior.
Define pass/fail thresholds before running verification, not after seeing the results.
Smoke tests and full pre-launch verification serve different purposes; smoke tests detect catastrophic failures, full verification assesses readiness across all risk dimensions.
Automate verification checks where possible; the cost of automation is paid back many times over in launch after launch.
Each verification category must have a named sign-off owner; the launch does not proceed until all sign-offs are recorded with supporting evidence.
